title: Operation TrueChaos: Chinese-Nexus Threat Actor Exploits TrueConf Zero-Day CVE-2026-3502 (CISA KEV, Deadline April 16) to Deliver Havoc C2 via Malicious Software Updates — Supply Chain Attack Compromises Southeast Asian Government Networks
affected_products: Any organization using on-premises TrueConf server deployments

sources.name: Check Point Research — Operation TrueChaos: 0-Day Exploitation Against Southeast Asian Government Targets
Check Point Blog — When Trusted Software Updates Become the Attack Vector: Inside Operation TrueChaos
CISA Alert — CISA Adds One Known Exploited Vulnerability to Catalog (TrueConf CVE-2026-3502)
BleepingComputer — Hackers exploit TrueConf zero-day to push malicious software updates
The Hacker News — TrueConf Zero-Day Exploited in Attacks on Southeast Asian Government Networks
The Record (Recorded Future) — CISA gives agencies two weeks to patch video conferencing bug exploited by Chinese hackers

SecurityWeek — TrueConf Zero-Day Exploited in Asian Government Attacks
SecurityOnline.info — TrueChaos: The TrueConf Zero-Day That Turned Secure Updates Into a Government Espionage Backdoor
Check Point Research — Amaranth Dragon: Threat Actor Context and Prior Havoc C2 Usage

timeline.event: Operation TrueChaos campaign begins; Chinese-nexus threat actors (assessed with moderate confidence as Amaranth Dragon / APT-41 overlap cluster) target Southeast Asian government entities using TrueConf as the delivery mechanism
CVE-2026-3502 publicly disclosed and NVD entry published; TrueConf releases patched Windows Client 8.5.3.884 with cryptographic integrity verification for software updates
Check Point Research publishes full Operation TrueChaos technical report detailing the supply chain attack methodology, DLL sideloading chain, Havoc C2 usage, and Chinese-nexus attribution evidence
BleepingComputer and SecurityWeek publish coverage. Malware hashes and C2 IP indicators published publicly.

CISA adds CVE-2026-3502 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with April 16 federal remediation deadline (BOD 22-01). The Record, Security Affairs, Help Net Security, SecurityOnline cover CISA KEV addition.
CISA federal agency patching deadline under BOD 22-01

simple_summary.what_happened: Researchers at Check Point uncovered a sophisticated espionage campaign (Operation TrueChaos) in which attackers exploited a zero-day vulnerability in TrueConf — a video conferencing platform popular in government and military organizations — to push malicious software updates to connected clients. The flaw (CVE-2026-3502) is that TrueConf's Windows client downloads and installs updates from the on-premises server without verifying the integrity or cryptographic signature of the update package. Attackers who gained access to a centrally-managed TrueConf server replaced the legitimate update with malware. Every TrueConf client connecting to that server then silently installed the malicious package, believing it was a trusted software update. The delivered malware established persistent access via the Havoc post-exploitation framework, enabling remote command execution and data exfiltration across multiple government agencies simultaneously — all through a single compromised server.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running TrueConf Windows Client versions 8.1.0 through 8.5.2 with on-premises TrueConf server deployments. TrueConf serves approximately 100,000 organizations globally, with strong adoption in government, military, defense, financial institutions, utilities, and critical infrastructure — particularly in Russia, CIS countries, and Southeast Asia. The campaign specifically targeted Southeast Asian government entities, but any organization with an on-premises TrueConf deployment and unpatched clients is at risk of similar attack methodology.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Threat actors gain access to a centrally-managed on-premises TrueConf server (initial access vector not publicly disclosed — likely phishing or exploitation of another vulnerability against the server). Step 2: Attackers replace the legitimate TrueConf update package on the server with a weaponized Inno Setup installer containing malware alongside legitimate TrueConf components to appear valid. Step 3: TrueConf clients query the server for updates and download the malicious package, installing it silently without any integrity verification — users see a normal update process. Step 4: The installer drops `poweriso.exe` (a legitimate binary) and malicious `7z-x64.dll` to `C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\`. When `poweriso.exe` loads, Windows DLL search order causes it to load the malicious DLL instead of the legitimate one. Step 5: The malicious DLL runs initial reconnaissance (`tasklist`, `tracert 8.8.8.8`) and exploits the Microsoft iSCSI Initiator (`iscsicpl.exe`) UAC bypass to escalate privileges. Step 6: The Havoc C2 framework is deployed, establishing persistent encrypted communication with attacker infrastructure on Alibaba Cloud and Tencent servers, enabling full remote control — command execution, process management, credential theft, and lateral movement across the compromised network.
what_you_should_do: Update all TrueConf Windows Clients to version 8.5.3.884 or later immediately — this version adds cryptographic integrity verification to the update process

Check for known malicious indicators of compromise (IOCs) on all systems that ran TrueConf: look for `C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\7z-x64.dll`, `C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\poweriso.exe`, and `%AppData%\Roaming\Adobe\update.7z`
Audit the TrueConf server for unauthorized access and review update package integrity — verify the hosted update matches the official TrueConf release hash

If compromise is suspected: isolate affected systems, conduct forensic investigation, and engage incident response — the Havoc C2 framework provides attackers with persistent access across all affected endpoints
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This is a classic software supply chain attack — one of the most effective techniques in nation-state espionage because it abuses the trust users place in legitimate software update mechanisms. A single compromised server can expose dozens or hundreds of client endpoints simultaneously. The targeted sectors (government agencies, defense ministries, critical infrastructure) are high-value espionage targets. CISA's addition to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a 14-day deadline reflects the severity assessment. The Havoc C2 framework gives attackers long-term, persistent access that survives reboots and continues to operate even after the initial malware delivery is detected. The attribution to a Chinese-nexus actor with connections to APT-41 (one of the most sophisticated state-sponsored threat groups) underscores the advanced nature of this campaign. Any organization that trusts software update mechanisms without cryptographic verification is vulnerable to the same methodology applied to other tools.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
tactics.description: Attackers compromised the on-premises TrueConf update server and replaced legitimate update packages with malware. The TrueConf client's lack of cryptographic verification (CWE-494) allowed the weaponized installer to be accepted and executed as a trusted update.

The malicious installer drops `poweriso.exe` (legitimate signed binary) alongside malicious `7z-x64.dll`. Windows DLL search-order causes the legitimate binary to load the attacker-controlled DLL, executing malicious code under the guise of a trusted process.

tactics.technique_name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control
tactics.description: Attackers exploit the Microsoft iSCSI Initiator (`iscsicpl.exe`) UAC bypass technique via DLL search-order hijacking with modified PATH environment variables to elevate from standard user to elevated privileges without a UAC prompt.

The Havoc C2 framework establishes encrypted command-and-control communications with attacker infrastructure hosted on Alibaba Cloud (43.134.90.60, 43.134.52.221) and Tencent (47.237.15.197), using application layer protocols to blend with normal traffic.

Initial post-exploitation reconnaissance includes `tasklist` execution to enumerate running processes and `tracert 8.8.8.8` to map network path — establishing situational awareness before deploying the persistent C2 framework.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on: (1) hunting for known malware artifacts and IOCs on systems running TrueConf, (2) blocking known C2 infrastructure, (3) identifying DLL sideloading of poweriso.exe in unusual directories, and (4) monitoring TrueConf server integrity. Given the campaign began in early 2026, affected organizations may have had persistent access established for weeks.

log_sources.description: Hunt for known malware file hashes, suspicious DLL loads by poweriso.exe, iscsicpl.exe spawning unusual child processes, and Havoc C2 communication patterns from TrueConf client systems.

Block and alert on connections to known C2 IPs: 43.134.90.60, 43.134.52.221, and 47.237.15.197. Monitor for other connections to Alibaba Cloud and Tencent IP ranges from endpoints that should not have reason to communicate with these providers.

Detect creation of `C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\7z-x64.dll`, `C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\poweriso.exe`, and `%AppData%\Roaming\Adobe\update.7z` on TrueConf client systems — these are key malware deployment artifacts.

Verify the integrity of update packages hosted on the on-premises TrueConf server. Compare SHA256 hashes of hosted update packages against official TrueConf release hashes. Any mismatch indicates tampering.
Monitor for `iscsicpl.exe` execution with unusual parent processes or modified environment variables. Monitor for DLL load events showing 7z-x64.dll loaded outside of 7-Zip installation directories.

what_to_look_for.description: MD5: 248a4d7d4c48478dcbeade8f7dba80b3. This is the primary malicious payload dropped by the weaponized TrueConf update installer.
MD5: 22e32bcf113326e366ac480b077067cf. This is the weaponized Inno Setup installer delivered via the compromised TrueConf update mechanism.
MD5: 9b435ad985b733b64a6d5f39080f4ae0. Used in the privilege escalation step via iscsicpl.exe DLL search-order hijacking.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Network connection to known C2 infrastructure
what_to_look_for.description: Outbound connections to Alibaba/Tencent-hosted C2 servers used by Operation TrueChaos. Block and alert immediately.
what_to_look_for.log_field: dest_ip IN ('43.134.90.60','43.134.52.221','47.237.15.197') AND src_process='trueconf.exe' OR src_process='poweriso.exe'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Artifact masquerading as Adobe: update.7z in Roaming\Adobe
what_to_look_for.description: Malware persistence artifact dropped at `%AppData%\Roaming\Adobe\update.7z` — legitimate Adobe software does not use this path.

immediate_actions.action: Update TrueConf Windows Client to 8.5.3.884 or later
immediate_actions.description: The patched version adds cryptographic integrity verification to the update download process, preventing malicious update substitution. Download the official installer directly from TrueConf's website — do NOT use the built-in auto-update mechanism until you have verified the on-premises server has not been compromised.

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for IOCs on all TrueConf client systems
immediate_actions.description: Immediately check all systems running TrueConf for the following malware artifacts. Presence of any of these indicates compromise requiring full incident response.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check for malware artifacts on Windows:
Get-ChildItem -Path 'C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Get-ChildItem -Path "$env:APPDATA\Adobe\" -Filter 'update.7z' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Get-FileHash 'C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\7z-x64.dll' -Algorithm MD5 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

immediate_actions.action: Block known C2 IP addresses at all perimeter controls
immediate_actions.description: Immediately add the following IPs to firewall block lists and EDR deny lists: 43.134.90[.]60, 43.134.52[.]221, 47.237.15[.]197. These are the confirmed Havoc C2 infrastructure IPs used in Operation TrueChaos.
immediate_actions.command:
# Windows Firewall block rules:
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Block TrueChaos C2-1" dir=out action=block remoteip=43.134.90.60
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Block TrueChaos C2-2" dir=out action=block remoteip=43.134.52.221
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Block TrueChaos C2-3" dir=out action=block remoteip=47.237.15.197

immediate_actions.action: Audit the TrueConf server for tampering
immediate_actions.description: Inspect the on-premises TrueConf server for unauthorized access and verify the integrity of hosted update packages. Compare SHA256 hashes of update packages against official TrueConf release hashes. Review server access logs for unusual logins, file modifications in the update directory, or configuration changes that could indicate the server was compromised as the initial pivot point.

immediate_actions.action: Initiate full incident response if IOCs are found
immediate_actions.description: If any malware artifacts (files, C2 connections, or malicious hashes) are found, treat the affected systems as fully compromised. The Havoc C2 framework provides persistent access — rebooting will not remove the malware. Isolate affected systems from the network, preserve forensic images, and engage your incident response team. Given the nation-state attribution, consider reporting to CISA (US organizations) or your national CERT.
